## [15:14 - 18:18]

responsive on a Tuesday and maybe you should email more on Tuesday. I had this experience back when I was publishing about the stock market and I used to send out an email every day at, at 4.30. It was after the market closed. So, market closes at 4pm. I'd sent out my email at 4.30. I just figured that's when I looked at email because after the market closed, I wasn't focused on the market, I would go look at email. That one of the things you have to remember is you are not your market and I was not my market just because I checked email at 4.30 and I thought it made sense to send an email at 4.30. It wasn't right because years later, it was actually after I got out of that business and I was building some other trading related sites for another reason and I just decided to do some testing on time of day because like in my old business, I didn't have the capability. I wasn't, you know, we were using a different mailing system so I couldn't split test. So, I split test and you know, I split test all different times throughout the day and it turns out a lot more people, a lot more traders would open their email if I sent it to them at 7.30 in the morning, you know, before the market opened than if I mailed after the market closed. I mean, it was a significant difference and you know, I'd been in that market for eight years just assuming 4.30 was a great time to mail and it was a horrible time to mail compared to 7.30. I would have made a lot more money. I could have added 10 or 20% to the bottom line of that business if I had just, you know, if I had just tested that, if I knew 7.30 was the time to mail. So, testing times can be really, really great. Testing day of the week can be really, really great. Email testing, super easy to do. It's even far easier. Now, one thing you want to look at when you're testing results is, I don't want to get too complicated, but basically the test, like when you look at open rates and click rates, it's dependent on the tracking, especially open rates. It's dependent on the a pixel showing up in the email reader that people are looking at. So, basically, different sort of like Gmail might change how they handle those tracking pixels and Yahoo might change and different programs might change how they track that stuff or how they load those pixels. So, if you're using a web or a profile or any service provider, you can't look at like, okay, my November open rate versus my July open rate, because things can technically change over time. You can only look at open rates and compare them when you do something like this, when they're sent fairly close together, a matter of minutes or maybe a matter of days. You can't say, boy, my open rate is lower than it was

## [18:19 - 18:58]

two years ago. Look at this. I'm getting this open rate and I had this open rate then. That's not an apples to apples. You can't do that. And then another big thing is you can only test one thing at a time. So, for this test, if you're testing the subject line, you can't also test the call to action. You can't also test putting a photo in there. That's a big time mistake that you see people make. You just cannot do that. You have to test one variable at a time. So, there you go. Some email copy tips and some email testing tips. These things will make a huge difference in the results that you get.
